Instructions Instructions Instructions Instructions Instructions Instructions Instructions Instructions Instructions The following risk assessment template is designed to assist with the execution of LEVEL 2 search warrants and other pre-planned events where forced entry is required. It is required to be completed by the member in charge of the entry and reviewed by a supervisor (Sergeant) prior to execution. The risk assessment provides generic areas of consideration to assist in the risk analysis process. It should be completed with the considerations of all stakeholders in mind and when all risks are identified, they must be rated using the likelihood and consequence tables and the risk matrix. The tables and matrix can be found in the Operational Safety, Tactics and Training (OSTT) intranet site.

If an extreme or high risk is identified where further treatment is required, a specific treatment plan should be completed. sections.t: Reasonable Grounds for Suspicion or Belief
Disposing of Property Seized Under Search Warrant
sections.h:
INTRODUCTION

A search warrant is a judicial document, which authorises police and in some cases persons assisting to enter various properties and places to search for and seize specified items, which would be admissible as evidence relating to an offence.

The primary function of a search warrant is the power to enter premises. The power to search is incidental to the power of entry.

‘A man’s home is his castle’ is still a valid maxim and the law regards a person’s home as their private domain in which they can do what they like with their own, without interference from anybody. It also regards their capacity to deny admission to anyone and provides measures for them to take action against trespassers and the like.

Search warrants may be obtained only in accordance with the various statutes, which authorise their issue. Likewise, there are a number of statutes controlling the manner in which police may execute such warrants.

The coverage in this drafting guide is mainly based on the law and the procedure for gaining an application. It will therefore be necessary to make such adjustments as are required by the specific Act you intend to operate under.

Whilst the following procedures relate specifically to the execution of search warrants, the principles, where practicable, may be applied to all searches including searches of persons, vehicles and premises with or without warrant.

Principally this document will relate to the physical execution of searches however for the sake of thoroughness, it will on occasion reference topics outside of that area.

Section 13 CRIMINAL INVESTIGATION ACT 2006 (WA) – APPLYING FOR A SEARCH

WARRANT

In Western Australia, the right to enter and search places, as well as search persons, is dependent upon:

• Statutory powers of search; and

• Very limited common law powers of search.

An application for a search warrant must be made to a Justice of the Peace (JP) in accordance with Section 13 Criminal Investigation Act (CIA). The application must be made on oath, unless the application is made by remote communication.

Since the JP must make a ‘decision’ based on the material in the application, in much the same manner as they would when determining if a defendant is guilty or not guilty, there must be sufficient factual evidence before the JP to justify the issuance of a search warrant.

It is a prerequisite to the issuance of a valid search warrant that the JP must be satisfied on the sworn evidence before them; firstly, that there are reasonable grounds for suspecting the presence of the things sought in the place, the object of the search, and secondly, that there are reasonable grounds for believing the same will afford evidence of the commission of the offence per Morse & Thompson v Harlock [1977] WAR. 65.

Therefore, it is not sufficient to just say “I reasonably suspect...” or “I reasonably believe...”

in the application.

Whilst the facts relied upon to establish reasonable cause must be set out in the written application, it is not necessary to repeat them on the face of the warrant.

The issue of a search warrant is not something that should be undertaken lightly as it makes lawful what would otherwise be a trespass, entry upon a person’s premises without their consent.

Issue of a Search Warrant is a ‘Decision’

When an application for the issue of a search warrant is properly made to a Magistrate or JP who has jurisdiction to issue the warrant, this person must decide upon the basis of the application made before them on oath, whether they will issue a search warrant or not. In other words, the Magistrate or JP must exercise their own judgement, not just rely on the oath of the police officer, but proceed judicially according to legal principles.

When issuing a Section 42 Search Warrant under the CIA the JP must follow Section 42(4) CIA. If a JP refuses to issue a search warrant, the JP must record on the application the fact of, the date and time of, and the reasons for, the refusal.

Only one JP is required to make a decision to issue a search warrant. Should the JP consider they have issued a search warrant improperly or by a mistake in a matter of fact reached the wrong decision, the JP may withdraw the warrant at any time per Barons v Luscombe [1933] 3 A.D. and B. 589.

sections.h:
It is recommended that all search warrants are prepared using the ‘Search Warrant’ section via the Investigative Standards Portal.

All applications for a search warrant must show the full name and official details of the person swearing the application.

In order to provide sufficient grounds for a JP to issue a search warrant, the party seeking the warrant must give evidence on oath before the JP.

When applying for a CIA search warrant the applicant must comply with Section 13 CIA.

(Warrants & orders, applying for)

The CIA search warrant application must be made in person before the judicial officer unless the warrant or order is needed urgently; and the applicant reasonably suspects that a judicial officer is not available within a reasonable distance of the applicant, in which case it may be made to a judicial officer by remote communication; and the judicial officer must not grant it unless satisfied.

Remote communication means any way of communicating at a distance including by telephone, fax, email and radio.

The application must be made in writing unless the application is made by remote communication; and it is not practicable to send the judicial officer written material, in which case it may be made orally; and the judicial officer must make a written record of the application and any information given in support of it.

The application must be made on oath unless the application is made by remote communication; and it is not practicable for the judicial officer to administer an oath to the applicant, in which case it may be made in an unsworn form; and if the judicial officer issues a warrant or order, the applicant must as soon as practicable send the judicial officer an affidavit verifying the application and any information given in support of it.

If on an application made by remote communication a judicial officer issues a warrant or order, the judicial officer must, if practicable, send a copy of the original warrant or order to the applicant by remote communication, but otherwise — the judicial officer must give the applicant by remote communication any information that must be set out in the warrant or order; the applicant must complete a form of a warrant or order with the information received and give the judicial officer a copy of the form as soon as practicable after doing so; and the judicial officer must attach the copy of the form to the original warrant or order and any affidavit received from the applicant and make them available for collection by the applicant.

For a warrant or an order to be made by remote communication there are two considerations that must be taken into account;

• The warrant or order is needed urgently

• The applicant reasonably suspects that a judicial officer is not available within a

reasonable distance of the applicant.

Therefore, the reasonable distance referred to will be relative to the urgency of the warrant or order.

Applying for warrants and orders are relatively straight forward under the CIA, however there are certain aspects of this section which officers need to be made aware of to ensure they are acting lawfully. Officers must comply with subsection (6)(d) and (7)(b) of Section 13 or any evidence obtained can be deemed inadmissible and require prosecutors to argue for the inclusion of that evidence under Section 155 of the Act.

The copy of the original warrant or order sent, or the form of the warrant or order completed, as the case may be, has the same force and effect as the original warrant or order.
sections.b: It is recommended that all search warrants are prepared using the ‘Search Warrant’ section via the Investigative Standards Portal. All applications for a search warrant must show the full name and official details of the person swearing the application. In order to provide sufficient grounds for a JP to issue a search warrant, the party seeking the warrant must give evidence on oath before the JP. When applying for a CIA search warrant the applicant must comply with Section 13 CIA. (Warrants & orders, applying for) The CIA search warrant application must be made in person before the judicial officer unless the warrant or order is needed urgently; and the applicant reasonably suspects that a judicial officer is not available within a reasonable distance of the applicant, in which case it may be made to a judicial officer by remote communication; and the judicial officer must not grant it unless satisfied. Remote communication means any way of communicating at a distance including by telephone, fax, email and radio. The application must be made in writing unless the application is made by remote communication; and it is not practicable to send the judicial officer written material, in which case it may be made orally; and the judicial officer must make a written record of the application and any information given in support of it. The application must be made on oath unless the application is made by remote communication; and it is not practicable for the judicial officer to administer an oath to the applicant, in which case it may be made in an unsworn form; and if the judicial officer issues a warrant or order, the applicant must as soon as practicable send the judicial officer an affidavit verifying the application and any information given in support of it. If on an application made by remote communication a judicial officer issues a warrant or order, the judicial officer must, if practicable, send a copy of the original warrant or order to the applicant by remote communication, but otherwise — the judicial officer must give the applicant by remote communication any information that must be set out in the warrant or order; the applicant must complete a form of a warrant or order with the information received and give the judicial officer a copy of the form as soon as practicable after doing so; and the judicial officer must attach the copy of the form to the original warrant or order and any affidavit received from the applicant and make them available for collection by the applicant. For a warrant or an order to be made by remote communication there are two considerations that must be taken into account; • The warrant or order is needed urgently • The applicant reasonably suspects that a judicial officer is not available within a reasonable distance of the applicant. Therefore, the reasonable distance referred to will be relative to the urgency of the warrant or order. sections.h:
A search warrant can only be issued where a criminal offence is involved, however, whilst there is some variation from statute to statute, warrants can be obtained where:

• An offence has been committed.

• An offence is suspected/believed to have been committed.

• It is suspected/believed an offence is being committed.

• It is suspected/believed an offence will be committed.

Applications to ground search warrants must clearly show one of the above categories in accordance with the provisions of the particular statute.

The offence for which the warrant is sought should also be described with sufficient particularity so as to leave the occupier of the premises in no doubt as to the reason for the search. Whilst the description will depend on what information is available at the time of applying for the warrant, it can be said that to simply insert terms such as ‘Stealing’ or ‘Burglary’ will not be sufficient.

Section 41(3)(c) CIA requires the Police Officer or Public officer to state ‘the grounds on which the applicant suspects that the offence has been or may be committed’.

Grounds on which the applicant On Monday 1 April 2019, Craig SMITH of 123 South suspects that the offence has Coastal Highway, Denmark WA reported to Police the been or may have been offence of Stealing, pursuant to section 378 Criminal committed s41 (3)(c) Code was committed on his place of business. Incident Report 010496 1000 7911 refers.

I have inquired into this information and believe it to be true.

This may be as simple as stating that a telephone call has been received from a complainant advising that an offence has been committed and an Incident Report submitted or that police have attended a scene and made observations which lead them to suspect an offence has been or may be committed. To satisfy particularity it is necessary to state the offence in full and the incident report number which relates.

sections.h:
A clear description of the place to be searched is essential. This should include the nature and situation, e.g., a house, giving the number, street, suburb or town. Where this cannot be done, it is advisable to mention the nearest cross street in suburban areas; or other fixed feature, such as kilometre post, where it is a country road.

If possible, the place to be searched should be observed before applying for a search warrant and, if practicable, a photograph taken so as to ensure an accurate description and/or a map can be produced to pinpoint its location.

In large buildings such as office blocks and hotels, the room or floor numbers may have to be included. Bear in mind some firms have a reception area on the ground floor, and offices or storerooms, on other floors.

Other tenants may occupy intervening rooms or floors. Where such details are not specific, such as a shanty in the bush, as clear a description as possible should be stated as to the location and type of building, e.g.:

Room 546, Hyatt Hotel 56 St Georges Terrace Perth, Western Australia 6000 Remember that an incorrect address will invalidate a warrant and cause a police officer to become classed as a trespasser. These principals are well enumerated in the case of (State v Deowraj Buddhu (2002) SC 1665).

sections.h:
Most search warrants are available for specific enactments to search for particular types of articles, e.g. Section 26 Firearms Act for firearms; Section 24 Misuse of Drugs Act for drugs, and those search warrants should be used accordingly.

Section 42 CIA grants wider powers of search in many respects, in that this type of search warrant is to look for ‘things relevant to an offence’ or to search the place for a ‘target person’ as distinct from certain items. However, it is advisable, wherever possible, to specify the property or evidence for which a search is desired per Jones v German [1896] 2 Q.B. 418.

Example (1): Should information be received that a certain person had entered a shop by forcing a window with a crow bar and stole a quantity of cigarettes (Section 401 Criminal Code), it would be sufficient to describe the property on a search warrant as:

A crow bar and a quantity of cigarettes (naming the various brands, if known).

Example (2): Where a search warrant is required to look for evidence relative to obtaining property by fraud (Section 409[1] Criminal Code), the property could be nominated as:

• One chequebook on (Bank and branch) containing cheques numbered from (first

number) to (last number) inclusive.

• Letter dated (specify if possible) from manager (or accountant) of (bank and branch)

advising account of (offender's name) is overdrawn.

• Receipt dated (specify) from (e.g. Myers Ltd.) for (specify amount).

• Gents suit, brown colour, size 105, ‘Parini’ make.

You will have noticed examples (1) and (2) relate to ‘things relevant to an offence’ which are likely to be located at the suspect’s premises. The ‘things relevant to an offence’ include property either stolen or obtained from both the complainants as well as articles used by the offender(s) to commit the offences. Item 2 in example (2) is a ‘thing relevant to an offence’ which will afford real evidence.

sections.h:
BELIEF

While reading the statutes you will notice a variety of terms in different sections indicating the state of mind a police officer must have before he or she can arrest any person, for example:

• Just cause to suspect

• Reasonable and probable cause for believing or suspecting

• Reasonable and probable cause for believing

• Reason to suspect

• Reasonably suspected

• Believes, on reasonable grounds

• Reasonable grounds for believing.

The same provisions apply to ‘Search and Seizure’. In fact, they apply on any occasion required by statute.

These can be separated into two categories:

1. Reasonable suspicion

2. Reasonable belief.

‘Reasonable’ means sound of judgement, sensible, moderate, not absurd, tolerable, fair.

‘Suspect’ means have an impression of the existence or presence of (something).

‘Suspicion’ means partial or unconfirmed belief (especially) that something is wrong or someone is guilty.

‘Belief’ means trust or confidence (in), acceptance as true or existing (of any fact, statement).

‘Believe’ means have faith in, put trust in truth of statement.

‘Faith’ means reliance, trust, belief founded on authority.

Therefore, ‘suspect’ is of a lower standard than ‘believe’ per Seven Seas Publishing Pty Ltd v Sullivan [1968] N.Z.L.R. 663.

The case of Hughes v Dempsey 17 W.A.L.R. 81, held that:

“Reasonable suspicion means that there must be something more than imagination or conjecture. It must be the suspicion of a reasonable man warranted by facts from which inference can be drawn, but it is something which falls short of legal proof.”

In other words, a police officer’s suspicion may be right, but then again, it may be wrong.

Provided such suspicion is reasonable it can be expected to be supported. This applies to exercising powers of search as much as to powers of arrest.

When applying for a CIA search warrant a police officer must have a reasonable suspicion.

Section 4 of the Act provides a definition of Reasonable Suspicion.

s.4 Criminal Investigation Act 2016, “Reasonably suspects”, meaning of:

For the purposes of this Act, a person reasonably suspects something at a relevant time if he or she personally has grounds at the time for suspecting the thing and those grounds (even if they are subsequently found to be false or non-existent), when judged objectively, are reasonable.

A good practical approach is to consider three general factors:

TIME:

• Would certain acts done in the night time be more suspicious than during the day

(and vice versa)?

• Was there only a short time between the offence and the cause for suspicion?

PLACE:

• How innocent is the person or place?

• Where is the person or place in relation to the offence committed?

CIRCUMSTANCES

• Were there furtive activities?

• Was the person/place/car of similar description?

• Did the person have the motive, means and opportunity?

Reasonable suspicion may be based on a police officer’s own observations, but evidence of independent witnesses is just as valid, and, as often as not, is better.

Suspicion can arise sometime after the offence is committed, but must be before effecting an arrest or search, as the case may be.

Hearsay evidence can be used as part of the grounds of reasonable suspicion, provided it is of ‘high reliability’ per Inglewood Servicemen's Club [INC.] v Mauriri [1981] N.Z.L.R. 882.

Documents such as incident reports or witness statements, which are sometimes hearsay evidence, may be incorporated as part of the written application to assist in establishing the required standard, i.e., reasonable suspicion or reasonable belief.

Damages may be awarded against police if suspicion is too easily aroused per Dumbell v Roberts [1944] 1 All E.R. 326 and McArdle v Egan [1933] 98 J.P. 103. In other words if a search is conducted, or an arrest is made, whether on warrant or not, and there were not sufficient grounds for reasonable suspicion (or belief), police could be liable civilly even though any charge laid following the search or arrest was successfully proved.

sections.h:
Prior to approaching a JP, a police officer must consult a Senior Officer (Sergeant or above, including Acting) independent of the inquiry to review the application for the issue of the warrant.

This addition to procedure has been implemented to ensure that a suitable system of supervision and managerial accountability exists and to ensure that members are aware of their obligations in relation to search warrants (refer to Police Manual SZ-01.03 Search Warrants)

When permission is granted by the reviewing police officer, the application for a search warrant can be presented to a JP.

sections.h:
The Australian courts will construe strictly the statutory authority for the issue of a search warrant, so as to provide safeguards for the rights of the individual. In this respect, the propositions expounded by Fox J. in R v Tillett; ex parte Newton (1969) 14 F.L.R. 101 emphasise the attitude in Australia of strict statutory compliance so as to save the search warrant from invalidity:

1. The JP when approached to issue a warrant must act as an independent authority,

exercising their own judgement and not automatically accepting the informant’s claim.

2. The JP has a discretion, which they must exercise judicially pursuant to rules laid

down by the judges. To enable this discretion to be properly exercised, the informant must place before the JP adequate and sworn evidence.

3. The warrant itself must clearly state the findings of the JP, as these findings will not

otherwise be assumed.

4. As a necessary corollary to the power of seizure, a particular offence must be

selected and specified both in the application and the warrant. This is so even where the statute simply uses the words ‘any offence’ and makes no clear reference to a need to specify the particular offence.

5. The warrant must not authorise the seizure of things in general or things, which are

related to offences in general. Thus, the warrant in referring to a particular offence should authorise seizure by reference to that offence.

6. The warrant may be struck down for going beyond the requirements of the occasion

in the authority to search.

7. The time for execution of the warrant must be strictly adhered to.

The above propositions relate to the validity of a search warrant. If the warrant is invalid then a superior court can take cognisance of the invalidity and quash the warrant by means of the prerogative writs of Certiorari (where the proceedings have been completed) or Prohibition (where it is sought to prevent the proceedings continuing).

The following passage, which is equally applicable in Western Australia, is an extract from the Australian Federal Police ‘Search Warrant Manual’ by Detective Inspector A.J. SING (November 1981):

“In recent years there have been many challenges against the validity of search warrants and the Courts will not hesitate to quash search warrants based on insufficient information, or because they are deficient for want of a particularity, failure to comply with the relevant statutory provision, lack of jurisdiction or failure to specify the offence alleged.

As a result, the framing of complaints and search warrants has become an art form, which demands a considerable amount of knowledge, skill and attention to detail. Generally, when a challenge is mounted against a search warrant the defence (law enforcement agency) will be limited to the written details on the complaint and warrant itself. Consequently, it is essential that all the information relied upon to support the issue of the warrant be included in the written complaint and the warrant itself is properly drawn.”

sections.h:
Whilst there are a number of legal points involved in the obtaining of a search warrant, there are five fundamental steps to be observed after initial introductions and informing the JP a search warrant is desired.

1. Police officer (complainant) takes the oath or affirmation.

2. Police officer (complainant) states or reads the application for requiring the search

warrant.

3. Police officer (complainant) signs the complaint.

4. JP signs the complaint indicating that the matters contained therein were sworn or

affirmed before them.

5. If satisfied with the application, JP signs the warrant.

The police officer completes both the ‘application for a search warrant’ and the ‘search warrant’ in full.

Taking the oath or affirmation entails the police officer holding up their right hand and saying:

I swear by Almighty God / Sincerely declare and affirm that the contents of this application are true and correct.

After taking the oath or affirmation, the police officer gives evidence on the grounds of suspicion in the complaint and accompanying statement.

The JP signs the complaint to signify it has been correctly laid before them. The JP can make notes on the complaint of any other grounds sought, or the reasons for their decision.

If the JP decides to issue the search warrant and agrees with its contents, they sign it. Again the JP can add to the warrant any conditions or restrictions he or she feels should apply:

• In the case of a CIA search warrant the JP should nominate the period, not exceeding

30 days, during which it may be executed; and

• If a JP refuses to issue a CIA search warrant, the JP must record on the application

the fact of, the date and time of, and the reason for refusal.

Where the search warrant requires an endorsement by the JP to authorise the search in the ‘night-time’ this should be added and signed by the JP.

The application for the search warrant should be placed in the case file.

sections.h:
Once a search warrant has been executed, a second search cannot be made using the old warrant. A fresh complaint must be sworn and a further warrant obtained.

When a warrant is directed to a particular person for execution, that person must execute it and has no power to delegate the authority conferred upon them per Symonds v Kurtz [1898] 53 J.P. 727. Normally they can use such assistance as is necessary for the purpose, e.g.

Section 14 Misuse of Drugs Act. Should a search warrant be addressed to ‘all police officers in the State...’ it means that any officer can execute it. In both instances, any police officer can swear the complaint to ground and obtain the warrant, but the police officer executing it should have it with them at the time.

Normally they can use such assistance as is necessary for the purpose, e.g. Section 14 Misuse of Drugs Act. Should a search warrant be addressed to ‘all police officers in the State...’ it means that any officer can execute it. In both instances, any police officer can swear the complaint to ground and obtain the warrant, but the police officer executing it should have it with them at the time.

sections.h:
When property is seized under the provisions of the Firearms Act it is to be taken forthwith before a JP, it need not be the JP who issued the warrant. When property is seized under the provisions of the CIA it is to be dealt with in accordance with Part 13 of the CIA and the Criminal and Found Property Act 2006.

INTRODUCTION

This manual contains the overarching principles relating to the execution of search warrants by the WA Police Force. Any police officer can execute a search warrant, whether it was applied for by them or another officer1.

Every member in charge of a warrant and every member involved in a warrant is individually responsible for their actions. This manual is an informing document that provides guidance for members in charge of a search warrant, supervisors and staff at all stages of development who seek to learn about search warrants.

Reference material relating to WA Police Force search warrants is located in the following areas:

• Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA) (CIA) s.41-45.

• Misuse of Drugs Act 1981 (WA) - (MDA) s.24.

• Firearms Act 1973 (WA) - (FA) s. 26.

• Weapons Act 1999 (WA) – (W/Act) s.14.

• Police Manual SZ-01.03 Search Warrants.

• Body Worn Camera (BWC) SOPs 8.2 Use of BWC During Search Warrants.

This document principally refers to the procedures required to comply with the CIA. Where specific legislation relates to MDA, FA or W/Act warrants, reference is made to the different procedures and relevant statute. Although many similarities exist when executing all types of warrants, members must know the legislative differences that apply in the relevant acts.

Warrant templates can be located:

• The WA Police Force CIA Forms intranet page, located here.

• The Investigative Standards intranet page under the heading, Search Warrants.

• Relevant legislation.

sections.h:
Obtaining the Warrant

Application for search warrants must meet legal requirements for the valid issue of the warrant. The Search Warrant Drafting Guide provides instructions that align with the legal requirements.

For approval relating to warrants for unoccupied premises and conditions for an independent person, refer to the unoccupied premises section, within this document.

1 s.43(5) CIA

For approval relating to planned covert execution under the provisions of the MDA refer to undisclosed search warrants within this document.

Planning and Responsibilities

Operational Order

In every case where members execute a search warrant, the member in charge shall ensure that an investigative operational order is prepared. The order will contain:

• A risk assessment of the premises and occupants.

• A statement of the roles and responsibilities for the:

▪ Member-in-charge of search

▪ Interviewing officer

▪ Physical material officer

▪ Video camera operator

▪ Searching officer

▪ Security officer

• A record of the briefing provided by the member in charge of the warrant or senior

investigating officer.

Member in Charge of Search

The member in charge of the search will ensure:

• The warrant is reviewed for correctness and is signed by a Justice of the Peace (JP).

• They are in possession of the warrant at the time of execution, unless the warrant is

obtained by remote communication.

• All members of the search warrant have reviewed the operational order, are aware of their

responsibilities and are aware of potential risks.

Physical Material Officer

The physical material officer will ensure a forensic exhibits kit2 is provided at each warrant.

Video Camera Officer

The video camera operator will ensure an approved electronic device used to audio-visually record the planned search warrant is charged and in working order.

Minimum Number of Officers

Six officers is the recommended minimum to conduct a safe, efficient and effective search.3 2 Refer to Forensic exhibit handling and packaging guidelines.

3 This does not preclude an officer in charge of a search from deploying fewer officers; for example, when executing a low risk search warrant to seize CCTV footage etc. The member in charge of the warrant will continually assess the risk, ensuring sufficient staff are provided to mitigate the risk.

Planned Time of Execution

The warrant is to be executed at a time appropriate to the legislation. Under s.43(6) CIA, a search warrant is to be executed between 6:00am and 9:00pm, unless the officer executing it reasonably suspects that if it were, the safety of any person, including the officer, may be endangered or the effectiveness of the proposed search may be jeopardised.

Under s.24(1) MDA, a search warrant can be executed at any time, however it is recommended all search warrants are executed between 6:00am and 9:00pm unless for the reasons above.

sections.h:
The execution of all search warrants and searches will be audio visually recorded4.

All members are to ensure searches are conducted in a thorough and professional manner and the integrity of the search is maintained at all times.

In general, search warrants may be executed by:

• Knocking and announcing. Planned lower risk warrant where it is planned to approach

the target place, and seek occupiers consent to enter by knocking on the door and talking to an occupant5.

• Tactical forced entry. Planned higher risk warrant where rapid tactical entry is

reasonably believed to be necessary, and occupiers rights are given to the occupant after an initial security search of the target place is complete. The policy for tactical forced entry is located at FR-01.08 Forced Entry to Premises.

A third type of warrant, a covert entry warrant is discussed later in this manual.

Body Worn Cameras - Entry

BWCs are to be turned on and recording prior to entering a target place6, until after occupier’s rights7 are given and the target place is secured.

After police enter and secure the target place, the member in charge of the search will coordinate the turning on of the audiovisual recording device and switching BWC to buffering mode.8

sections.h:
8 SOP 8.2 Use of BWC in Search Warrants

Occupier’s Rights s.31(2) CIA

CIA occupier’s rights are an essential consideration of any warrant. The officer in charge must consider the requirements of the legislation under which the warrant was issued. For example:

• ‘Occupier’s Rights’, s.31 CIA are to be provided to all occupants in CIA search warrants.

Rights will generally be given before entry unless the member in charge of the search reasonably suspects that to do so will endanger any person, including the officer, or jeopardise the purpose of the proposed entry and or the effectiveness of any search of the place9.

• In MDA Warrants there is no legislative requirement to provide occupiers rights found at

s.31 CIA. It is recommended that where practicable, the same rights outlined in s.31 CIA are given to occupiers where an MDA warrant is executed. This recommendation does not apply to MDA warrants where covert entry is made. The decision and rationale to provide or not to provide occupier’s rights rests with the member in charge of the MDA warrant.

Knock and Announce Entry

BWC is to remain activated until occupier’s consent is obtained and the premises is secured10.

Officers will11:

• Identify themselves to the occupier. If in plain clothes, produce police identification.

• Inform the occupier that it is intended to enter the place.

• If the place is to be entered under a search warrant, give the occupier a copy of the

warrant.

• If the place is to be entered under some other statutory authority, inform the occupier of

the reason, and the statutory authority, for the entry

• Give the occupier an opportunity to give informed consent to the place being entered.

Occupier’s consent only extends to allowing police to enter the premises. The CIA search warrant gives police the power to both enter and search the premises. Regardless of occupier’s consent, police will execute the search warrant.

Use of Force

If entry is refused, reasonable force may be used to gain entry12.

Security

Once entry is made, secure the target premises and assemble those present in a suitable area. Safety and security of police, suspects, witnesses and members of the public is to be a primary consideration at all times.

9 s.31(2) CIA

10 SOP 8.2 Use of BWC in Search Warrants

11 s.31 CIA

12 s.16 CIA

Tactical Forced Entry

A tactical forced entry may be necessary when an officer reasonably suspects that if they were to enter in compliance with s.31(2) CIA (Occupier's Rights), such entry will endanger any person and or may jeopardise the proposed entry or effectiveness of the search.

Where an officer intends to conduct a tactical entry, they must be justified in doing so. For all search warrants, officers are encouraged to consult their supervisor or an independent senior officer prior to executing a tactical entry. A “Forced Entry Risk Assessment” form must be completed where it is known a forced entry will occur.

Before consideration of forced entry is made to premises, the member in charge shall ensure:

• The correct target address is confirmed.

• The necessity to use force is considered.

• The amount of planned force is reasonable.

• The vulnerabilities and age (especially children) of all occupants is considered.

• All members are dressed in contemporary personal protective equipment as prescribed

by Operational Safety & Tactics Training Unit (OSTTU).

• Damage is kept to the minimum required to affect the entry.

• The necessity for Senior Officer Orders for adjacent premises is considered.

In cases of high risk, officers may request the Tactical Response Group (TRG) to assist in the entry. Refer to High Risk Entry and SS-7.2.2 TRG Call-Out and Deployment Procedures.

• Upon arrival operate BWC in accordance with BWC SOP 8.2.

• Enter the premises in accordance with Method of Entry procedures available from OSTTU,

using approved method of entry equipment available on RMIS.

• Once entry is made, secure the premises and assemble those present in a suitable area.

• As soon as possible after police have entered and secured the target place, provide

occupiers rights to the occupant.

Safety and security of police, suspects, witnesses and members of the public is to be a primary consideration at all times. 9 s.31(2) CIA 10 SOP 8.2 Use of BWC in Search Warrants 11 s.31 CIA 12 s.16 CIA Tactical Forced Entry A tactical forced entry may be necessary when an officer reasonably suspects that if they were to enter in compliance with s.31(2) CIA (Occupier's Rights), such entry will endanger any person and or may jeopardise the proposed entry or effectiveness of the search. Where an officer intends to conduct a tactical entry, they must be justified in doing so. For all search warrants, officers are encouraged to consult their supervisor or an independent senior officer prior to executing a tactical entry. A “Forced Entry Risk Assessment” form must be completed where it is known a forced entry will occur. Before consideration of forced entry is made to premises, the member in charge shall ensure: • The correct target address is confirmed. • The necessity to use force is considered. • The amount of planned force is reasonable. • The vulnerabilities and age (especially children) of all occupants is considered. • All members are dressed in contemporary personal protective equipment as prescribed by Operational Safety & Tactics Training Unit (OSTTU). • Damage is kept to the minimum required to affect the entry. • The necessity for Senior Officer Orders for adjacent premises is considered. In cases of high risk, officers may request the Tactical Response Group (TRG) to assist in the entry. Refer to High Risk Entry and SS-7.2.2 TRG Call-Out and Deployment Procedures. • Upon arrival operate BWC in accordance with BWC SOP 8.2. • Enter the premises in accordance with Method of Entry procedures available from OSTTU, using approved method of entry equipment available on RMIS. • Once entry is made, secure the premises and assemble those present in a suitable area. • As soon as possible after police have entered and secured the target place, provide occupiers rights to the occupant.

sections.h:
After entry is made and the target place is secured, occupier’s rights are given and occupants are secured in a suitable area, the legal status of all occupants must now be considered.

Obtain personal details from each occupant and decide their status.

Arrested Suspect – s.128 CIA

Ensure the Arrested Suspect is provided their CIA s.137 and s.138 rights.

Record the time and place the rights were given. Ensure the arrested suspect is cautioned and has a full understanding of each section of the police caution and their rights.

Accompanying Person – s.28 CIA

People accompanying officers to be informed of their rights – s.28 CIA. This applies to all search warrants regardless of the legislative power.

Record the time and place the rights were given. Ensure the accompanying person is cautioned and has a full understanding of each section of the police caution and their rights.

If there is any uncertainty whether the accompanying person may become a suspect, provide all of their rights at s.137 and s.138 CIA, with the exception of telling them they are under arrest. This will safeguard any subsequent confessional evidence.

Detain and Search Persons Present

s.43(8)(b)(ii) CIA authorises an officer to conduct a basic or strip search of a person who is in the target place when the warrant is being executed for the target thing.

s.24(1) MDA specifies that any person present can be detained and searched during a search warrant, using such force as necessary.

If necessary to search an occupant of the premises the video camera is to remain on during the course of the body search. In the case of a strip search the camera is to be pointed away so as not to visually record the body search.

The officer conducting the search is to provide an audio commentary of what is occurring so as to allow the audio to be recorded. If any items of interest are located they may be photographed in the position they are found on the body.13

sections.h:
Member in Charge of Search

The member in charge of the warrant is expected to have sufficient experience or supervision to lead a search warrant. The member in charge of the search should oversee the search and not involve themselves in physically searching the target place. They will exercise overarching control of the search, delegating tasks where appropriate and ensuring the lawful collection of evidence. The member in charge of the search will take all necessary steps to minimise the risk of harm to all people present.

After entry the member in charge will consider:

• Inviting the occupant to declare any prohibited or dangerous items, or those items

prescribed in the warrant.

• Ensure where possible that the occupant views the search14.

13 s.65(4) CIA

14 s.31 CIA

• If the occupant is not present or declines to accompany police, the member in charge of

the search will stop the search and comply with the process outlined in the unoccupied premises and independent person section.

• Do not place the occupant or independent person in a position where they must observe

police search more than one room (or area) at a time15.

• Conduct a systematic (room to room, one room at a time) search of the target place.

• Where a room or area is locked, or access is made difficult because the occupant has

secured a room, request the occupier’s assistance to access the room (keys, code) before using force to gain entry.

• Prior to stopping an audiovisual recording ensure a time and date is announced.

• When an audiovisual recording is recommenced, ensure a time and date is announced,

and provide a short commentary on what occurred whilst the camera was switched off.

Invite the occupier to comment. Remind the occupier of their rights and the police caution.

• Sign and date the execution of the search warrant on both the occupiers copy and the

original.

• File the endorsed warrant with the application on the CRIME file.

• Ensure you retain possession of the original warrant so it can be produced in court if

required.

• Ensure that forced entry repairs or damage to any property is dealt with appropriately.

Audiovisual Recording of Search Warrants

s.45(2) CIA states that if reasonably practicable, an audiovisual recording must be made of the execution of the search warrant. Officers will audio visually record all search warrants, unless exceptional circumstances exist. Where a recording is not made, officers must be prepared to justify the absence of that recording to the court.

Generally, it is not practical to video record an entire search warrant, therefore intermittent recording should be adopted. Where intermittent video recording is utilised, on each occasion recording is re-commenced, the officer in charge will give a short commentary on what occurred whilst the camera was switched off and invite the occupier to comment.

Where a search is not audio visually recorded, the officer entering the execution of a search warrant on IMS will briefly outline the reasons for the absence of any audiovisual recording of the search in the free form text area of that system.

All members participating in the search are to be mindful of inappropriate language or comments made during search warrants. Poor language, comments or behaviour by police can unnecessarily prejudice the admissibility of evidence. Officers are to take great care and remain professional at all times.

All search warrant recordings are copied to disc and placed onto IMS property as a Search Warrant Recording, including search warrants recorded on One Force mobile phones. This 15 If there is more than one occupant, this would not preclude arranging for sufficient staff to conduct more than one room search simultaneously, observed by individual occupants.

disc is sent to the Video Interview Suspects Unit as a master for storage, in the same manner as a suspect interview16.

Video Camera Operator

The nominated camera operator will record the warrant. The camera operator's duties include:

• Ensuring the audiovisual equipment is functional before deploying to the search location.

• Ensuring the time and date showing on the video camera is correct.

• Ensuring the time and date is recorded. If the time and date is not correct or is not

functioning, consider changing the video camera. If this is not a viable option explanation of the incorrect date and time should be stated on the recording at the earliest opportunity.

• The camera operator will record the current condition of the target premises by conducting

a pan of the premises before searching commences, and at the conclusion of the search, in company with the occupier and security officer.

These procedures outline the minimum requirement for the recording of searches. Where deemed appropriate and practicable, additional parts of the search or the entire search may be recorded. The audiovisual recording of the search shall include:

• An introduction of all members assisting during the search warrant to the occupier.

• A commentary of all occupier’s rights and introductions provided.

• The police caution and check of understanding.

• The production of the search warrant.

• A request to declare any prohibited items such as prohibited drugs.

• A request to declare any dangerous items secured or unsecured that pose a potential

occupational health and safety risk to occupiers or police involved such as hypodermic syringes.

• A request to declare any weapons secured or unsecured such as firearms.

• A request to declare any large sums of money or items of value such as jewellery or gold.

• A request to declare any items particularised in the warrant.

• An explanation to the occupier of the procedure to be adopted during the search.

• Any items located during the search, which are to be seized.

• The counting of any money located during the search.

• The sealing into Security Movement Envelopes (SMEs) of any money, items of value or

drugs.

• Any comment made by the occupier relating to items located during the search.

• The process where the physical material officer identifies and shows the items seized to

the occupant, providing them with an Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log (Copy), including the return of any items to the occupier.

• Any damage caused during the entry to the premises or the subsequent search.

• Any complaint made by the occupier.

• While recording, if a recording is interrupted for any technical reason (battery, fault), upon

resumption of recording an explanation is to be recorded.

16 Police Manual SZ-01.03 and ER-01.01

Searching Officer

Great care is to be taken when searching for evidence. Experience has shown when searches are rushed or not systematic, evidence can be overlooked. Personal safety is to be exercised when searching and members should take every opportunity to use appropriate personal protective equipment.

Searchers will employ a mindset that considers the protection of all forensic evidence in accordance with forensic exhibit handling procedures. If physical material is located and a video recording device is not on, if the officer is wearing BWC they should immediately activate their BWC, back capturing the previous 30 seconds, including the moments when they discovered the item. The member in charge of the search will coordinate the switching of BWC to buffering mode once the video camera operator recommences recording.

Physical Material Officer

• Direct the video camera operator to video or photograph seized property in situ prior to

moving the item. This is particularly important if the weight or nature of an exhibit is likely to make production in court impractical.

• Packaging of seized items will comply with Forensic Exhibit Handling and Packaging

Guidelines.

• Every item seized is to be recorded on the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log,

noting the location of where it was found. Where possible use the description of the room as specified by the occupant in the initial pan of the premises.

• Where no property or drugs are seized the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log

shall be endorsed “Nil Seized”.

• Ensure that seized money, items of value or drugs are recorded.

• SMEs are used to secure seized money, items of value or drugs.

• At completion of the search, the physical material officer will:

▪ Review the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log.

▪ Identify items located during the search which are to be seized.

▪ Return any items not to be seized to the occupier.

• A copy of the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log is to be provided to the

occupier. Invite the occupier to sign the log as a true and accurate record of the items seized.

Securing the Premises After the Search

At the completion of a search, where no person is to remain at the premises, the member in charge shall ensure the premises are left secure.

If the premises are unable to be secured, arrangements shall be made for a contractor to attend and affect the necessary repairs prior to police departure. Where damage has occurred effecting entry or during the search, such damage shall be photographed or audio visually recorded.

Forced Entry Repairs

At the completion of a forced entry search, where no person remains at the premises, the member in charge of the search must ensure that the premises are left secured. The following procedure will assist officers when securing premises:

• If the premises are unable to be secured, arrangements should be made in the

metropolitan area through the District Operational Supervisor (DOS) or in Regional WA (RWA), the relevant District Office for a service provider to attend and affect the necessary repairs prior to police departure.

• Upon receiving the following information, DOS or RWA equivalent will arrange for the

service provider to attend. The requesting officer will provide:

▪ Name, rank and regimental number.

▪ Station/unit.

▪ CAD incident job number.

▪ Nature of the damage.

• Payment by WA Police for forced entry repairs shall only be authorised where:

▪ Temporary repairs are required in order to secure premises.

▪ Unnecessary or unreasonable force was used to gain entry.

▪ No offender or evidence was found and no charges result from the search.

▪ An innocent third party is financially disadvantaged as result of damage caused on

entry or during the search.

sections.h:
Seizure of Property

• All seized property is to be recorded onto an Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material

Log.

• The original Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log (white copy) is to be retained

with the CRIME file/brief, the duplicate copy (pink) is to be given to the occupier and the triplicate copy (yellow) is to remain in the book for Good Governance.

• Property details recorded on the Property Receipt/Physical Material Log are to be entered

onto IMS as soon as practicable17.

• All seized property shall be correctly labelled and include the IMS number and IMS

barcode.

• All seized property shall be stored securely and correctly prior to transfer to the Exhibit

Management Unit (EMU). Seized property is not to be stored in desks, filing cabinets or the general office areas of your working area.

• All movements of seized property shall be recorded on IMS.

• At the completion of the court hearing, all seized property shall be dealt with in accordance

with the court order.

• All receipts for the return of property shall be filed with the officer-in-charge.

17 PR-1.1.3 Recording and Entering Property in Police Possession Seizure of Drugs

• All drug seizures shall be sealed in SMEs or a vented bag and secured with a security tag.

• The securing of the prohibited drugs into the SME is to be audio visually recorded.

• The SME shall be marked with a description of the seizure and the signature and

regimental number of the exhibit officer and correctly recorded onto the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log.

• The occupier or an independent person shall be requested to sign the SME.

• Subsequent weighing of drugs at a police station will be done in accordance PR-01.02.

All seized drug (and paraphernalia) property shall be correctly entered on IMS.

‘Clan Lab’ responses are located in the Clandestine Drug Laboratories section of this document.

Seizure of Money

• Audio visually record and photograph in situ any money located.

• In the presence of the occupier, count the money and place it into an SME.

• If the volume of money is substantial, package the money into an SME and use the ‘money

serial recorder’ located at the Drug & Firearm Squad.

• If the volume of money presents a handling problem, additional envelopes shall be

obtained or a commissioned officer may arrange for another secure method of transport.

• The occupier shall be requested to read aloud the envelope numbers.

• All seized money shall be correctly entered onto IMS.

• All SMEs containing money shall be stored in a safe or another secure location designated

for that purpose.

• All seized money shall be banked in the Police Suspense Account unless it is required for

some legitimate forensic purpose. The receipt shall be filed with the officer-in-charge.

• Prior to banking any seized money or dealing with it in any way it shall be photographed

in a manner to record type, denominations and consideration should be given to recording individual serial numbers.

• When any money is banked, IMS shall be updated.

Opening Security Movement Envelopes (SMEs)

• When SMEs containing drugs or other items seized are opened for any reason, the whole

procedure shall be audiovisually recorded in the presence of two officers.

• The SME shall be cut open at the bottom, so that the seals are left intact. The member

cutting open the SME shall endorse the time, date and location of opening the envelope and sign it. A witnessing member shall also sign the envelope. The reason for the opening the envelope should be audio visually recorded.

• Upon re-sealing the contents into a new SME, the opened envelope shall also be sealed

in the new envelope in a manner so that the serial number is clearly visible.

• Both officers are to sign the new SME.

• Record the number of the second SME on the item record within the IMS, together with

the date and time of opening, the reasons for opening, and the names of the authorising supervisor, the officer opening the SME, and the witnessing officer.

• Audiovisual recordings used to record the opening of SMEs and the processing of physical

material shall be stored at the recording office/station.

• For further information, refer PR-01.02.1.1.

sections.h:
Unoccupied Premises and Independent Person

Considerations

When entry is affected and the premises are found to be unoccupied, the member in charge shall:

• Immediately withdraw all staff from the premises.

• Advise a commissioned officer who may nominate an independent person (sergeant or

above) to be present during the search.

• Ensure no search is conducted until the arrival the nominated independent person.

• No approval shall be granted unless it is shown that it is necessary to conduct the search

in the absence of the occupier or owner.

• Where the premises are unoccupied the member in charge must leave the following in a

prominent position:

▪ A notice stating the officer’s official details and that the place has been entered.

▪ If entry was gained by search warrant, a copy of that warrant.

▪ A copy of the Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log.

▪ If entry was obtained by the use of another statutory authority, the reason, and the

statutory authority, for the entry.

Independent Person Guide

Independent persons are appointed to ensure and observe compliance with search warrant procedures. They are to ensure impartiality. They will not make operational decisions or have specific involvement in the search or seizure. Should a situation arise that requires intervention from the independent person, they shall bring this matter to the attention of the member in charge of the search.

Independent persons must:

• Be nominated by an authorising commissioned officer.

• Hold the rank of sergeant or above (includes a member officially acting in that position or

rank).

• Where practicable, not be from the case officer’s organisational unit.

• Ensure the search warrant is sighted and correct.

• Be present throughout the entirety of the search.

• Monitor compliance and ensure the search is conducted in a professional manner.

Upon arrival, and at commencement of their role, the following statements should be used as a guide, and is to be audiovisually recorded before recommencement of the search:

“I am a nominated Supervisor, from [business unit], appointed by [Name of Authorised Commissioned Officer], as the independent person for the execution of this search warrant. My role is to monitor compliance with regards to Legislation, WA Police Force Policy and Standard Operating Procedures. I have sighted the search warrant which has been approved by a Justice of the Peace, and will ensure any physical material seized during the execution of the search warrant is recorded onto an Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log.”

Investigative Interviewing

All interviews, whether recorded at the search warrant location, police station or other location are to be conducted in accordance with the WA Police Force investigative interviewing model.

A preliminary interview of a suspect at the location does not negate the need for an audiovisual record of interview upon return to the police station.

Clandestine Drug Laboratories

In the event of discovery of a clandestine drug laboratory, the member in charge of the warrant will cease all activity and withdraw all personnel to a safe distance18. Initial action includes:

• All Police personnel and suspects depart the site and remain at a safe distance in case of

fire, explosion and/or chemical contamination.

• No ignition sources are taken into the site.

• The scene will be preserved and Drug & Firearm Squad are to be notified immediately.

• No attempt is made to shut down any on-going chemical process or remove a power

source to the premises without the advice of a Forensic Chemist.

• Where possible provide the Forensic Chemist with a description of the laboratory set-up,

including whether the laboratory appears to be operating at the time.

• Do not re-enter a laboratory for any of the above purposes.

• After the Forensic Chemist has rendered the site safe, photographs and audiovisual

recordings of the scene may be made, where appropriate.

Proceeds of Crime Considerations

The Proceeds of Crime Squad (PCS) conduct investigations into matters pursuant to the Criminal Property Confiscation Act 2000 (WA) (CPCA) and may commence confiscation proceedings where:

18 CR-07.05 Clandestine Drug Laboratories

• A person has been charged with a serious drug offence and has the potential to be

declared a drug trafficker pursuant to s.32A(1) of the MDA.

• A person has property directly or indirectly used in or derived from a confiscable offence

(i.e. punishable by two or more years imprisonment) including being in possession of large amounts of unexplained cash.

• The apparent wealth of individuals or criminal organisations cannot be adequately

explained.

PCS can assist during the planning stage of a search warrant by compiling or providing financial / asset profiles of the target and should be contacted in all instances at the earliest opportunity if there is any potential the target person may be charged with:

• A serious drug offence likely to render them a drug trafficker under the MDA; or

• In any other Act, where property is crime used (i.e. a house used for cannabis

cultivation or a vehicle used in a sex assault) or crime derived (i.e. paid for with the proceeds of crime).

If, during a search warrant, large amounts of cash or assets reasonably suspected to be crime used or crime derived are located, contact PCS. PCS will provide advice and may attend the location. The powers under the CPCA are “here and now” powers and must be executed while the target is in possession of the property, during the search warrant. Charging the suspect prior to PCS conducting a compulsive interview will likely prevent a compulsive interview being conducted and result in potential loss of evidence.

For more information on PCS guidelines refer to the Proceeds of Crime Investigation Manual located on the Investigative Standards intranet site.

High Risk/Terrorist Incident

The deployment of TRG for all other incidents should fall within the roles and responsibilities of the TRG as outlined in SS-07.02 and will be at the discretion of the OIC TRG or the Police Tactical Group (PTG) Commander.

All personnel requesting the TRG for planned operations shall submit a Tasking Request Form and Investigators Target Summary as located in the WAPOL Intranet under the SID T&CG Process. Requests must be approved at District Inspector or Divisional Inspector level prior to being forwarded to the TRG.

The TRG will respond to all incidents classified high-risk as determined by the provisions of SS-07.02 Tactical Response Group (TRG).

Undisclosed Search Warrant – s.24 Misuse of Drugs Act

s.24 MDA Undisclosed Search Warrants should only be considered under exceptional circumstances and where operationally necessary. Where it is necessary to conduct a planned search covertly on a premises pursuant to s.24 MDA, the Drug & Firearm Squad is to be notified.

Use of Detection Dogs

• The Canine Section is able to provide the following specialist resources:

▪ Narcotic detection dogs (NDDs) for the searching of premises, vehicles, vessels etc.

▪ Passive alert drug detection dogs (PADs) for the mass screening of passengers on

public transport, patrons in entertainment districts and at special events.

• Where practical, a Tasking Request Form (TRF) is to be submitted to the Canine

Operations Deployment Supervisor. Dynamic requests will be accepted by telephone or by Station Mail email.

• The Canine Section can be contacted 24/7 via 9370 7368 and all out of hours requests

must be submitted to SOCC.

• Under no circumstances are WA Police Force members to approach other agencies such

as the Australian Border Force (ABF), Australian Federal Police (AFP) or the Department of Justice (DOJ). Protocols have been established between these external agencies to utilise all resources appropriately. These agencies will only consider the use of their dog resources at the request of the OIC Canine Section.

• After entry has been effected, and before searching commences, the dog handler shall

instruct the occupier of the procedure to be adopted during the search.

• Before searching commences the handler and the dog may examine the premises in the

presence of the occupier or independent person and a nominated member of the search team.

• Any positive reaction shown by the dog shall be brought to the attention of the member in

charge of the search before the actual search is conducted.

• The area or object of positive reaction shall be searched in the presence of the occupier

or an independent person in accordance with these procedures.

CIA Search Warrant Ancillary Powers

A search warrant issued under the provisions of the CIA also authorises the officer executing it to exercise any or all of the ancillary powers found at s.44 CIA. Officers are required to be fully conversant with the powers conferred under a search warrant within the relevant Act prior to executing the search warrant.

Adjacent Premises – Senior Officer Order

s.44(2) CIA search warrant ancillary powers makes provision for police to enter but not search a place near the target place if an officer reasonably suspects it is necessary to do so in order to prevent a thing from being concealed or disturbed, prevent a person fleeing a target place, or to protect the safety of any person, including the officer, who is near the target place.

s.44(3) CIA states that this power cannot be exercised without senior officer approval. Senior Officer orders are located here.

sections.h:
GUIDE

Officers using this quick guide are to refer to the Investigative Standards Search Warrant Manual. This guide is not comprehensive and is intended as a ready reckoner for members in charge of searches, to refresh their memories relating to key evidentiary benchmarks during a search warrant. This document omits key considerations found in the manual.

Immediately Prior to Entry:

• The IO is to have the signed Search Warrant in their physical possession

• Check correct address / time / particulars.

Occupiers Consent:

• BWC activated – occupant advised

• Provide CIA occupier’s rights

• Give a copy of search warrant to the occupant

• Member in charge will coordinate BW activation and deactivation (to buffering mode).

Evidence Collection:

• Introduction of member in charge. Time, date, location (recorded on camera)

• Name, address and DOB of occupants

• CIA Arrested Suspect / Accompanying Person Rights

• Check of Understanding

• Cash or valuables / items posing a risk of harm

• Audiovisual pan of premises

• Question to Occupant – are the items listed in the warrant here?

Search:

• Advise occupant of search process

• Question to Occupant – do you have any questions?

• Systematic search – room by room

• Locate item - activate BWC, recording the last 30 secs

• Member in charge coordinate camera operator to commence audiovisual recording

and deactivation (buffering mode) of searching officer’s BWC

• Member in charge to revisit caution, rights and check of understanding when

audiovisual device is turned back on

• Relevant investigative interviewing questions*.

Search Complete:

• Physical Material Officer to provide copy of exhibits register

• Record and address any complaints

• Question to Occupant – any questions?

• Pan of premises

• Time and date - announce time and completion of warrant.

*Note – this does not necessarily negate the need for audiovisual record of interview upon return to police station.

sections.b: Officers using this quick guide are to refer to the Investigative Standards Search Warrant Manual. This guide is not comprehensive and is intended as a ready reckoner for members in charge of searches, to refresh their memories relating to key evidentiary benchmarks during a search warrant. This document omits key considerations found in the manual. Immediately Prior to Entry: • The IO is to have the signed Search Warrant in their physical possession • Check correct address / time / particulars. Occupiers Consent: • BWC activated – occupant advised • Provide CIA occupier’s rights • Give a copy of search warrant to the occupant • Member in charge will coordinate BW activation and deactivation (to buffering mode). Evidence Collection: • Introduction of member in charge. Time, date, location (recorded on camera) • Name, address and DOB of occupants • CIA Arrested Suspect / Accompanying Person Rights • Check of Understanding • Cash or valuables / items posing a risk of harm • Audiovisual pan of premises • Question to Occupant – are the items listed in the warrant here? Search: • Advise occupant of search process • Question to Occupant – do you have any questions? • Systematic search – room by room • Locate item - activate BWC, recording the last 30 secs • Member in charge coordinate camera operator to commence audiovisual recording and deactivation (buffering mode) of searching officer’s BWC • Member in charge to revisit caution, rights and check of understanding when audiovisual device is turned back on • Relevant investigative interviewing questions*. Search Complete: • Physical Material Officer to provide copy of exhibits register • Record and address any complaints • Question to Occupant – any questions? • Pan of premises • Time and date - announce time and completion of warrant. *Note – this does not necessarily negate the need for audiovisual record of interview upon return to police station.
